title: Flock Management & Multi-Species Care
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

supplementary.title: [Reference] Your local extension poultry management publications

competence_markers.description: The student manages a small animal flock independently through at least six months including one significant challenge
The student manages at least two species simultaneously with species-specific care routines
The student can evaluate feed labels and explain the relationship between nutrition and animal product quality
The student has managed reproduction in at least one species (hatching or kindling)
The student integrates animals with the garden or orchard for pest management and fertility
The student maintains and reviews production and health records regularly
The student can explain predator management systems and has successfully prevented predator losses

content.why_this_matters:
You have cared for animals daily. You know the routine, you can handle them correctly, and you understand that their needs come first. Now you step up to a fundamentally different level: you are not just caring for animals — you are managing them. The difference matters.

A caretaker follows a routine someone else designed. A manager designs the routine, solves problems, makes decisions, and takes responsibility for outcomes. When a hen stops laying, the caretaker reports it. The manager investigates: Is she molting? Is the feed right? Is she being bullied off the feeder? Is she sick? Is she old? The manager diagnoses, decides, and acts.

This packet also introduces you to a second species. Managing two different kinds of animals simultaneously teaches you that what works for chickens does not work for rabbits, that ducks have different needs than either, and that competent animal husbandry requires species-specific knowledge — not generic "animal care." Every species has its own biology, its own behavior, and its own management requirements.

You will also learn to integrate animals into the land system — moving chickens through the orchard to manage pests, using animal manure as a fertility resource, and designing your animal management to improve the land rather than degrade it. This is where small animal care becomes regenerative agriculture.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Independent flock management** — the student manages a poultry flock through all seasons without daily adult direction
2. **Multi-species competence** — managing at least two small animal species with species-specific knowledge
3. **Nutrition understanding** — feed composition, protein requirements, and the relationship between feed quality and animal product quality
4. **Reproduction management** — understanding the reproductive cycle, incubation or gestation, and care of young animals
5. **System integration** — using animals in the garden and orchard for pest management, fertility, and land improvement
6. **Record keeping** — maintaining production, health, and management records as the foundation of competent animal husbandry
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Poultry Management — Becoming Independent

You already know the daily routine. Now you learn the systems behind it.

**Housing Design and Ventilation**

The number one killer of backyard chickens — more than predators, more than disease — is poor ventilation in the coop. A coop needs airflow. Chickens produce moisture from their breath and droppings. In a sealed coop, that moisture condenses on cold surfaces, creates damp conditions, and causes frostbite and respiratory disease. The instinct to seal a coop tight in winter is the instinct that kills chickens.

Proper ventilation means openings near the roofline that allow moist air to escape while keeping drafts off roosting birds. Birds can handle very cold temperatures (chickens routinely survive -20 degrees Fahrenheit) as long as the air is dry. They cannot handle damp cold.

Design principles:
- Ventilation openings at the highest point of the coop (hot, moist air rises)
- No drafts at roost level (birds sleeping in a wind tunnel get frostbitten combs and feet)
- Hardware cloth (not chicken wire) on all openings — chicken wire keeps chickens in but does not keep predators out
- Minimum 4 square feet of floor space per bird inside the coop, 10 square feet per bird in the outdoor run
- Roosts higher than the nest boxes (chickens seek the highest point to sleep; if nest boxes are higher, they sleep in the nest boxes and soil them)

**Predator Management**

Every region has predators that will kill your chickens if given access. The list varies by location but commonly includes: raccoons (can reach through 1-inch mesh and pull birds out piece by piece), foxes (will dig under fences), hawks (will take birds from open runs in daylight), weasels (can fit through openings as small as 1 inch), neighborhood dogs (the most common predator of backyard poultry in suburban areas), and snakes (take eggs and small chicks).

Predator-proofing requires:
- Hardware cloth (1/2 inch mesh) on all openings — not chicken wire
- A buried apron of hardware cloth or welded wire extending 12-18 inches outward from the base of the coop and run to prevent digging
- A secure door that latches firmly — raccoons can open simple hook-and-eye latches
- Overhead protection or covered runs for hawk-prone areas
- A closing routine: every bird inside, every door latched, every night. No exceptions.

**Seasonal Management**

Chickens behave differently through the year:
- **Spring:** Increasing daylight triggers increased egg production. Hens may go broody (wanting to sit on eggs and hatch them). Parasite pressure increases as temperatures warm.
- **Summer:** Heat stress is a real risk above 90 degrees Fahrenheit. Provide shade, adequate water, and ventilation. Egg production may dip in extreme heat. Watch for signs of heat stress: panting, wings held away from body, lethargy.
- **Fall:** Decreasing daylight triggers molt — hens lose and regrow feathers. Egg production drops significantly or stops. This is normal, not a health problem. Increase protein in the diet during molt to support feather regrowth.
- **Winter:** Shortest days mean lowest production. Some hens stop laying entirely. Supplemental lighting (14-16 hours total light) maintains production but is optional — some keepers allow a natural winter rest. Prevent waterers from freezing. Monitor for frostbite on combs and wattles.

### Part 2: Second Species — Expanding Your Competence

Managing a second species forces you to think in species-specific terms. What works for chickens does not automatically work for rabbits or ducks.

**If your second species is rabbits:**
- Rabbits are hindgut fermenters — they extract nutrition from fiber through a specialized cecum. They produce two types of droppings: hard pellets (waste) and soft cecotropes (partially digested material they re-eat directly from their anus to extract additional nutrients). This is normal and essential — do not prevent it.
- Rabbits are sensitive to heat. Above 85 degrees Fahrenheit, rabbits are at risk of heat stroke. Shade, frozen water bottles, and good ventilation are critical in summer.
- Rabbit housing must be clean and dry. Wire-bottom cages allow droppings to fall through but can cause sore hocks (foot injuries) if wire gauge is too small. Provide solid resting boards.
- Rabbit manure is one of the few animal manures that can be applied directly to garden beds without composting first — it is mild enough not to burn plants. This makes it extraordinarily valuable for garden fertility.

**If your second species is ducks:**
- Ducks are waterfowl. They need access to water deep enough to submerge their heads — this is not optional, it is a health requirement (they clean their nostrils and eyes by dunking). A rubber livestock tub works.
- Ducks are messier than chickens with water. Housing design must account for wet conditions. Deep litter with straw manages moisture.
- Duck eggs are larger, richer, and better for baking than chicken eggs. Duck egg production is seasonal — many breeds lay heavily in spring and taper off by fall.
- Ducks are the best slug control available. A few ducks in the garden in the evening will eliminate slug populations that nothing else touches.

### Part 3: Nutrition — What You Feed Determines What You Get

Animal feed is not a generic commodity. The quality and composition of what you feed directly determines the health of the animal and the quality of what it produces.

**Protein matters most.** Laying hens need 16-18% protein in their diet. Meat chickens need 20-24%. Growing chicks need 18-22%. Molting hens benefit from increased protein (20%+) to support feather regrowth. A hen fed insufficient protein produces fewer eggs with thinner shells.

**Calcium is critical for laying hens.** Eggshell is almost entirely calcium carbonate. A laying hen needs calcium supplementation beyond what feed provides — offer free-choice oyster shell or crushed limestone.

**Read feed labels.** Every bag of commercial feed lists its protein content, fat content, and fiber content. Learn to read these labels and understand what they mean. Compare the protein content of different feeds. The cheapest feed is often the lowest protein — and the false economy of cheap feed shows up in reduced production and increased health problems.

**Supplemental feeding and foraging.** Chickens on pasture or in a garden obtain a significant portion of their nutrition from insects, seeds, and green plants. This supplemental foraging improves both the nutritional quality of their eggs (pastured eggs have higher omega-3 content and more vitamin D) and the animals' welfare. A chicken that spends its day scratching and foraging is healthier and less aggressive than one confined to a bare run with only commercial feed.

### Part 4: Reproduction — Understanding the Cycle

**Chicken reproduction:**
A hen does not need a rooster to lay eggs. She needs a rooster only if you want fertile eggs that can hatch into chicks. If you want to hatch chicks:
- Introduce a rooster to the flock (one rooster per 8-12 hens is typical)
- Collect fertile eggs and incubate them (21 days for chicken eggs) using an incubator with controlled temperature (99.5 degrees Fahrenheit) and humidity, turning eggs at least three times daily — or allow a broody hen to sit on them, which she does naturally and usually better than any incubator
- Chicks need a brooder: a warm, draft-free enclosure with a heat source starting at 95 degrees Fahrenheit and decreasing by 5 degrees per week until they are fully feathered (about 6 weeks)
- Chick starter feed (18-22% protein) for the first 8 weeks, then transition to grower feed

**Rabbit reproduction:**
Rabbits are famously prolific. A doe (female) can produce a litter every 30-31 days with litters of 4-12 kits. Gestation is 31 days. The doe builds a nest and lines it with fur she pulls from her chest. Kits are born blind and hairless and are completely dependent on the doe's milk for the first 2-3 weeks.

Do not breed rabbits unless you have a plan for the offspring. Unplanned rabbit reproduction produces more animals than most families can house or feed within a few months.

### Part 5: Integration with Garden and Orchard

Small animals are not just food producers — they are land management tools when used correctly.

**Chicken tractors:** A portable, bottomless enclosure moved across garden beds between crops. Chickens inside scratch, eat insects and weed seeds, and deposit manure directly on the soil. Move the tractor to fresh ground every 1-2 days. By the time the chickens have worked a bed, it has been fertilized, weeded (partially), and de-bugged — ready for the next crop with minimal work from you.

**Orchard integration:** Chickens under fruit trees eat pest insects (codling moth larvae, apple maggot pupae) that overwinter in fallen fruit and leaf litter. They scratch through the litter, breaking up pest habitat and adding fertility. This is one of the most effective organic pest management strategies for tree fruit — and the chickens feed themselves while doing it.

**Duck slugging:** Ducks released in the garden in the evening eat slugs voraciously. They are the single most effective slug control method available to the organic grower. Time their garden access to avoid damage to tender seedlings — ducks will trample young plants.

**Rabbit manure direct-application:** Unlike chicken manure (which must be composted to avoid burning plants), rabbit manure can be applied directly around plants. Scatter it on garden beds as a gentle, continuous fertility source.

### Part 6: Record Keeping — Knowing What Is Actually Happening

Competent animal management requires records. Not because someone is checking your homework, but because without records, you do not know what is actually happening — you only think you do.

**What to track:**
- **Daily egg count** — total and by bird if possible. Track trends over weeks and months.
- **Feed consumption** — how much feed are you going through per week? Per bird? This number tells you whether your animals are eating normally.
- **Health events** — any illness, injury, treatment, or abnormal observation, with date and outcome.
- **Mortality** — any death, with date and likely cause. A pattern of losses tells you something specific about your management.
- **Management events** — coop cleaning, bedding changes, deworming, predator incidents, housing modifications.
- **Reproduction** — breeding dates, hatch dates, number hatched, survival rate.

Review your records monthly. Patterns emerge that daily observation misses. A hen whose production has slowly dropped over three months may need nutritional changes. A flock with recurring respiratory issues may need better ventilation. The records tell the story that memory distorts.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Independent Management
**Developing well:** The student manages daily and seasonal animal care without direction. They identify and solve problems independently — adjusting ventilation in winter, providing heat mitigation in summer, modifying feed during molt. They anticipate needs rather than react to crises.

**Developing slowly:** The student still waits for direction. They handle the routine but do not adapt when conditions change. Problems that require judgment rather than routine stump them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student checking on animals outside the routine schedule? A competent manager visits the animals at odd times — not just morning and evening — to observe behavior at different times of day. A student who only interacts during the care routine is still in caretaker mode.

### 2. Multi-Species Management
**Developing well:** The student manages two species with distinct routines, can articulate how the species differ in their needs, and coordinates management across both. They adjust their schedule to accommodate both species without neglecting either.

**Developing slowly:** The second species receives less attention than the first. The student treats both species identically rather than adapting management to species-specific needs.

**Diagnostic questions:** Can the student explain three specific ways the two species differ in their management requirements? If not, they need more species-specific study. Assign reading from the species-specific reference and then have them identify the management differences.

### 3. Nutrition Competence
**Developing well:** The student reads feed labels, understands protein and calcium requirements, adjusts feed for life stage and season, and can evaluate whether their feeding program is producing good results (egg quality, body condition, feather quality).

**Developing slowly:** The student fills the feeder with whatever feed is available without evaluating its suitability.

**Diagnostic questions:** Hand the student two feed bags with different labels. Can they identify which is appropriate for laying hens and which for growing chicks? Can they explain why?

### 4. Record Keeping
**Developing well:** Records are current, consistent, and reviewed regularly. The student can answer management questions from their records and has identified at least one trend or pattern from record analysis.

**Developing slowly:** Records are sporadic, incomplete, or never reviewed. The student does not see the connection between recording and managing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Ask the student to calculate their cost per dozen eggs from their records. If they cannot, the records lack the data needed for basic management analysis. Redesign the record-keeping system together.

### 5. System Integration
**Developing well:** The student uses animals deliberately in the garden or orchard — chicken tractor rotations, duck slug patrol, manure collection and application. They can describe the benefits to both the animals and the land.

**Developing slowly:** Animals and garden/orchard are managed as separate systems. The student has not attempted integration.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student have the infrastructure for integration — a chicken tractor, portable fencing, a plan for moving animals through the garden? If not, building the infrastructure is the next step. Integration requires equipment and planning before it requires skill.
